body_txt: A woman is topless or fully naked, and the writers don't want to (or can't) stick an object into the scene to cover her up . Fortunately, her hair is somehow just long and thick enough to preserve her modesty. Sometimes Nipple and Dimed is at play, with the hair only being enough to cover the nipples. Named for Lady Godiva, an eleventh-century Anglo-Saxon noblewoman who rode a horse through the city of Coventry to protest an oppressive tax. Legend has it that she rode naked; whether that means literally nude or "stripped" in some metaphorical sense is academic, since most artistic representations show the lady covered only with her long, flowing hair. note On a side note, most of the citizens agreed to look away as she passed and not shame her; later, in a thematic painting, one man is shown stealing a look, who supposedly was struck blind for this. This is the origin of the term "peeping Tom" . Mermaids and similar half-human beings are typically depicted like this if the writer doesn't want to use an Organic Bra or Seashell Bra . The rare male example uses his facial hair to cover his naughty bits, but the sheer length of beard required generally restricts this use to old hermits or shipwrecked castaways, while fantasy settings could see dwarves or gnomes pulling this off more feasibly given their shorter stature. A character with Prehensile Hair might do this on purpose. May be combined with Censor Shadow , Censor Steam , Censor Suds , Hand-or-Object Underwear , or Scenery Censor . See Shoulders-Up Nudity if her hair is covering her nipples, yet her waist is left bare but unseen by the camera. Related to the Theiss Titillation Theory (after all, what will happen if she turns her head just a bit too far, or the breeze picks up...?). Can go with Hair as Cultural Identity is their long hair is part of their customs, traditions, and practices. Related to Hem Length Censorship .
